demographic_anchor.name: Marcus Trevino
demographic_anchor.education: associate degree in kinesiology + physical therapist assistant certification, Houston Community College
demographic_anchor.employment: full-time — physical therapist assistant, outpatient clinic, Houston Medical Center area

life_stories.title: The Waiting Room Education
life_stories.when: age 26-28, first years in clinical work
life_stories.event: Marcus's patients are mostly elderly — post-op recovery, chronic pain management, stroke rehab. He spends 7-8 hours a day doing hands-on manual work with people in their sixties and seventies who watch cable news all day and want to talk about it. He has heard every Medicare policy rumour, every Social Security scare story, every 'did you hear what they're doing now' opener from patients waiting for their session. He became fluent in the anxieties of heavy news consumers without ever becoming one himself. He learned that knowing the specific story was less important than being able to hold the emotional register — so he got good at responding to news anxiety without actually tracking the news.
life_stories.lasting_impact: His professional relationship with news is almost entirely vicariously absorbed from anxious elderly patients. This reinforces his sense that active news consumption produces anxiety without utility. He is sympathetic to his patients without sharing their habits.

life_stories.title: The March Madness Benchmark
life_stories.event: Marcus follows college basketball and the Houston Rockets with near-professional intensity. He has ESPN app notifications on, refreshes beat reporters' Twitter accounts during the trade deadline, reads detailed statistical breakdowns of box scores. Sports is where his information appetite lives — he can describe the last three years of the Rockets' front office strategy in detail. The cognitive bandwidth he devotes to sports is exactly the bandwidth he does not devote to general news. He made this trade consciously at some point in his early twenties — not as a political statement, just as a function of how much attention he has available.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Sports media consumption has crowded out general news consumption almost entirely. He is not resistant to information — he is an active, high-engagement information consumer in one specific domain. This makes avoidance hard to dislodge: it is not laziness or low literacy, it is genuine domain prioritisation.

life_stories.title: The Caseload Cutoff
life_stories.when: age 27, after a difficult three-month caseload stretch
life_stories.event: Marcus went through a period where his clinic was understaffed and he was carrying 28 patients per week instead of the standard 20. The emotional labour of working with people in pain was high — one patient was in hospice-adjacent care and it was heavy. He noticed that on evenings after brutal sessions, scrolling through news on his phone extended the low-grade tension he was already carrying. He experimented with keeping his phone in his bag on the drive home and not turning it on until after dinner. He went three weeks without checking any news beyond sports. At the end of those three weeks he felt measurably better, and his sleep improved.
life_stories.lasting_impact: He now treats news avoidance as an emotional regulation tool, specifically during high-caseload periods. He has repeated the three-to-four-week news blackout four times in the past year. He mentions it matter-of-factly if it comes up — not as a principled stance, just as something he does.

fear_appeal_responsiveness.label: moderate — desensitised by patient-absorbed news anxiety

information_need.label: high — but domain-specific (sports); near-zero for general news

news_anxiety_score.label: moderate-high — primarily experienced as emotional spillover from patient care

emotional_labour_fatigue.label: high — physically and emotionally demanding clinical work leaves limited bandwidth

budget_consciousness.label: high — $46k in Houston is comfortable but not loose

peer_influence_strength.label: moderate — news consumption not a strong social driver in his peer group either

perceived_time_scarcity.label: high — physically demanding job, commute, social life

routine_rigidity.label: moderate-high — structured around clinic schedule and sports seasons

news_app_usage.label: near-zero — no general news apps; ESPN and NBA app only

avoidance_frequency.label: often — 3-4 week blackouts multiple times per year; news is optional by default

avoidance_driver.label: primary driver: protecting limited emotional bandwidth after high-demand clinical work

platform_hierarchy.label: sports media dominant; general news absent

news_subscription_status.label: none — no paid general news subscriptions (ESPN+ for sports)

youtube_news_use.label: near-zero for news; uses YouTube for sports highlights and workout content

podcast_news_use.label: near-zero for news; listens to sports podcasts regularly

coping_mechanism.description: Marcus manages news exposure through domain substitution — his information appetite is satisfied by sports media, leaving no cognitive vacancy for news to fill. During high-stress clinical periods, he explicitly deploys a phone-off protocol on his commute home, producing 3-4 week news blackouts multiple times per year.

key_tensions: High information appetite and engagement with media in the sports domain — but identical content consumption behaviours simply do not exist for general news
Empathetic professional who holds space for patient anxiety about policy news all day — but deliberately does not absorb that anxiety himself
Treats news avoidance as an emotional regulation tool; behaviour is pragmatic rather than principled — could shift if emotional calculus changed

narrative:
I work with people's bodies all day. By the time I'm done with my last patient — usually someone who just had a knee replaced or is relearning how to walk after a stroke — I've been on my feet for eight hours and I've been fully present for every one of those sessions. My patients are mostly older, mostly retired, and most of them have CNN or Fox News on all day and they want to talk about it. I hear about every Medicare rumour, every Social Security story, every thing their sister texted them about what the government is doing now. I listen. I respond. I hold space for it.

And then I get in my car and I don't turn my phone on until I get home.

That started as an experiment about a year ago during a stretch where I had way too many patients and some of them were in really rough shape — one was basically in end-of-life care and it was heavy. I noticed that if I opened my phone right after those sessions, I was carrying the weight of whatever I just saw plus whatever the news was doing. I tried just not doing that. Not for any principled reason. Just to see how I felt. I felt better. Sleep improved. So I kept doing it.

I'm not disconnected from the world. I follow the Rockets obsessively — I know their last three years of front office decisions, every rotation player's numbers, I have ESPN notifications on. That's where my attention lives. General news feels optional to me. If something major happens, someone will tell me — my roommate, my mom, someone in the group chat. I've gone three, four weeks without checking any news multiple times this past year and I didn't miss anything I actually needed to know.
decision_bullets: News consumption is optional by default — not built into daily routine; no pull toward building it in.
Sports media fully occupies the information engagement bandwidth that news might otherwise fill — displacement effect is total.
Emotional bandwidth after clinical work is consciously protected; any content that extends emotional burden is deprioritised.
3-4 week blackout periods are well-established, repeating behaviours — completely unreachable as a news consumer during these windows.
Word-of-mouth from roommate, mother, or friend group is the only realistic re-entry point for breaking news.
Will engage with news if direct personal relevance is clear (patient policy impact, housing cost, sports industry news) but does not maintain background monitoring.
No news subscriptions; ESPN+ satisfies the paid media appetite ceiling.
Institutional trust in media is low-moderate; credibility alone will not activate consumption — personal relevance or peer referral required.

core_memory.identity_statement: Houston PT assistant who conserves emotional bandwidth by treating general news as optional; sports media is his primary information identity.

life_defining_events.event: Started working with elderly patients who are heavy news consumers — absorbed their news anxiety vicariously every day
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Sees active news consumption as anxiety-producing rather than utility-producing; patient experience reinforces avoidance

life_defining_events.event: High-caseload period led to phone-off commute experiment — went 3 weeks without news; sleep and mood improved
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Treats news blackouts as an emotional regulation tool; repeats them 3-4 times per year during stressful clinical stretches

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: $46k gross — limited discretionary spend; ESPN+ is the paid media ceiling
non_negotiables: emotional bandwidth protection after clinical work

brand_memories.espn: daily use — primary information platform; notifications on
brand_memories.nba_app: regular use — real-time scores and news
brand_memories.twitter_x: used primarily for sports accounts; general news accounts not followed
brand_memories.general_news_outlets: no regular use; no accounts followed; no app installed
